title: The Proprietary Trap and the Summit That Wasn't
subhead: The day's real technology-power story unfolded across two continents: a Beijing summit that revealed the limits of AI diplomacy, and a Senate hearing that exposed how proprietary lock-in has captured America's special operations forces.

sections.heading: The Summit Without Chips
sections.body:
Donald Trump arrived in Beijing with Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang and Elon Musk in his delegation, but the optics masked a deeper structural reality. The first US presidential visit to China in nearly a decade placed AI at the center of talks between the world's two dominant powers — yet substantive commitments remain unlikely because the technology itself has become the contested terrain.

Stanford's 2026 AI Index Report shows the US-China model performance gap has effectively closed. US and Chinese models traded the lead several times since early 2025, with DeepSeek-R1 briefly matching top US models in February. By March 2026, the top US model led by only 2.7%. The old assumption of unassailable American AI superiority is almost over.

Huang's presence was significant not for what it signaled about cooperation, but for what it revealed about the bind Nvidia faces. The company hit a $5.4 trillion market cap on May 13, yet its stock has underperformed its own sector for months. The reason: China exposure. Huang has warned that the day DeepSeek runs best on Huawei hardware would be a 'horrible outcome' for American interests. That day is approaching. Huawei is projected to capture 60% of China's domestic AI market by end of 2026. Nvidia took a $4.5 billion write-down on H20 inventory — chips designed to skirt export bans that Chinese firms are increasingly rejecting in favor of domestic alternatives.

The summit's subtext: both countries understand that AI is now infrastructure — determining how economies grow, states govern, militaries fight, and information moves. But the mechanisms for managing this competition remain rudimentary. Trust is thin. Washington accuses Beijing of model 'distillation' and cyber espionage; Beijing presents its AI progress as domestic innovation. The two leading AI powers are building competing stacks with minimal communication on safety standards, military use, or crisis protocols.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for both powers would be establishing real AI communication channels on model theft, talent flows, data-center infrastructure, and military applications. The trap: treating AI as just another diplomatic file rather than the infrastructure that now runs through every uncertainty in the world order. If Washington and Beijing fail to build rules of the road, smaller powers absorb the consequences.

sections.heading: TSMC's Arizona Expansion: More Capital, Same Dependency
sections.body:
TSMC approved a $20 billion capital increase for its Arizona operations, adding to the $31.28 billion capex approved for global expansion. The Arizona fab will boost output by 80% this year while its Japan facility surges 130%. But the structural reality remains unchanged: the most advanced nodes — the 2nm chips that every wafer through 2026 has already claimed — stay in Taiwan.

The CHIPS Act reshored political narrative, not cutting-edge capability. TSMC's five 2nm fabs under construction are all in Kaohsiung, Taiwan. Every 2nm wafer through 2026 is already sold out. Arizona produces older nodes — valuable for automotive and industrial applications, but not for the frontier AI training that matters most for power.

This creates a geographic chokepoint. Taiwan remains the sole source for the most advanced semiconductors. Arizona gives the US political cover and some domestic capacity, but the strategic dependency persists. The more the US invests in Arizona, the more it reveals what it couldn't secure: leading-edge production where it matters.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for the US would be accepting that reshoring older nodes reduces supply chain risk but doesn't eliminate the Taiwan dependency — then planning accordingly. The trap: believing the CHIPS Act solved the problem when it only repositioned it. A Taiwan crisis would still crater advanced AI development regardless of Arizona's output.

sections.heading: The Proprietary Trap: US Special Operations Locked Out of Their Own Equipment
sections.body:
In a Senate Subcommittee hearing, US special operations commanders revealed a structural vulnerability with no easy fix. Manufacturers' proprietary agreements prevent operators from modifying or upgrading their own equipment — especially unmanned systems — as adversaries iterate faster.

Lt. Gen. Lawrence Ferguson, chief of Army Special Operations Command, testified: 'We are bound right now to the actual vendor of that system that has the proprietary capability.' Lt. Gen. Michael Conley of Air Force Special Operations Command noted that adding capabilities often hits 'walls' when large vendors lock mission computers. Drug cartels and groups like Al-Shabaab can acquire and modify small drones easily; US special operators cannot.

Right-to-repair provisions were stripped from the fiscal 2026 National Defense Authorization Act. The companies that built the proprietary moats retain control. Meanwhile, technology evolves faster than the acquisition system can respond. This is not a procurement problem — it's a sovereignty problem. The state has ceded operational authority to contractors.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move would be legislative action mandating source-code access and modification rights for defense systems. The trap: assuming proprietary lock-in protects intellectual property when it actually degrades combat capability. The cost of inaction is a military that cannot adapt at the speed of conflict because its own equipment is legally locked against it.

sections.heading: China's Battery Dominance: BYD Surpasses Tesla in Energy Storage
sections.body:
BYD shipped over 60 GWh of battery energy storage systems in 2025, surpassing Tesla's 46.7 GWh to claim the top spot among global BESS integrators. Eight of the top 10 BESS integrators are now Chinese companies. China installed 65 GWh of large-scale battery storage in December 2025 alone — more than the entire United States deployed all year.

BYD's vertically integrated model gives it a structural cost advantage Tesla cannot replicate. BYD manufactures its own Blade LFP battery cells. Tesla buys its Megapack cells from CATL and BYD — its direct competitors. The HaoHan system offers nearly three times Tesla Megapack's capacity per unit. The 12.5 GWh Saudi deployment with Saudi Electricity Company is one of the largest grid-scale battery projects in the world.

This is not just an energy story. It's an industrial base story. Grid-scale storage determines who can integrate renewables at scale, who controls the infrastructure for the energy transition, and who builds the technical capacity for utility-scale systems. China is executing across all three.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for Western nations would be industrial policy that builds domestic vertical integration rather than subsidizing demand. The trap: assuming solar and wind capacity equals energy sovereignty when the storage infrastructure that enables intermittency is Chinese-controlled. The grid-scale storage market is not going to wait for Western alternatives.

sections.heading: Europe's LNG Dependency: Trading Russian Gas for American
sections.body:
The Institute for Energy Economics and Financial Analysis projects that US LNG will reach 80% of European LNG imports by 2028, up from 58% last year. Europe traded one dependency for another — a geopolitical risk concentration dressed up as diversification.

The $750 billion EU commitment to buy US energy commodities over three years, signed last year, created a structural tie. The European Parliament has problems with the deal. Trump threatened tariff hikes unless the bloc signs as-is. The arrangement elevated American LNG in Europe's supply mix, but the physical and financial challenge of supplying that volume remains unaddressed.

This matters beyond energy. European industrial competitiveness depends on stable, affordable power. American LNG prices are higher than Russian pipeline gas was. The more Europe leans on US LNG, the more it imports American energy politics, American LNG export capacity constraints, and American pricing power.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move would be aggressive renewable and heat pump deployment to reduce total gas demand while building storage and nuclear for baseload. The trap: believing LNG diversification means energy security when it means supplier concentration. Europe's energy sovereignty is being negotiated in Washington, not Brussels.

sections.heading: Ukraine's Ground Robots: Infantry Replacement or Asymmetric Necessity?
sections.body:
Ukraine's 100th Mechanized Brigade used a ground robot carrying 300 kilograms of explosives to destroy a building occupied by Russian troops in Kostiantynivka — the first documented kamikaze ground robot assault. President Zelensky has tasked defense officials with ensuring a supply of at least 50,000 UGVs this year.

Ground robots are moving beyond logistics and evacuation into direct assault missions. Manpower shortages drive adoption: Ukraine is fighting an asymmetric war against a larger military while facing mobilization challenges. UGVs can carry larger explosive payloads than aerial FPV drones, making them useful against fortified positions and urban structures.

But the technology is not invulnerable. A Ukrainian soldier estimated that 75-80% of UGVs are destroyed by enemy FPV drones, not jamming. Ground robots equipped with fiber-optic control links resist electronic warfare. The integration of ground and aerial systems — combined arms warfare by unmanned means — is still evolving.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move is building the manufacturing base to produce UGVs at scale while developing doctrine for combined unmanned operations. The trap: assuming robots replace infantry rather than creating new vulnerabilities. The side that integrates ground and aerial unmanned systems faster — with robust anti-drone countermeasures — will define the next phase of land warfare.

sections.heading: Private Influence Operations: The BlackCore Investigation
sections.body:
French authorities are investigating whether an obscure Israeli firm called BlackCore conducted a foreign interference campaign targeting France Unbowed (LFI) candidates ahead of March municipal elections. The campaign allegedly deployed deceptive websites, social media accounts, and digital ads — actions Meta, Google, and TikTok identified as 'coordinated inauthentic behavior.'

BlackCore described itself as 'an elite influence, cyber, and technology company built for the modern era of information warfare.' The company has since taken its website and LinkedIn offline. Reuters could not verify where BlackCore was based or find it in Israeli corporate records. French intelligence is investigating who commissioned the operation.

The operation targeted candidates in Marseille, Toulouse, and Roubaix. QR codes were posted around Marseille pointing to a fake blog alleging sexual misconduct against one candidate. The methods are familiar from state-sponsored influence operations, but the actor is allegedly a private firm selling services to unknown clients.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move would be robust disclosure requirements for influence-technology firms operating across borders. The trap: treating this as a foreign interference story when it's also a privateering story — influence operations as a service. The market for election manipulation tools exists. The question is who buys, who sells, and whether any jurisdiction can regulate it.

bottomLine: The day's developments reveal a consistent structural pattern: the concentration of technological power in fewer hands — whether Nvidia's $5.4 trillion valuation, Chinese battery dominance, American LNG market share, or proprietary control over military equipment — creates dependencies that geopolitical actors must navigate or break. The Trump-Xi summit placed AI at the center of great power competition, but the more telling story was what couldn't be negotiated: the hardware stacks, the model ecosystems, the manufacturing capacity. TSMC's Arizona expansion adds capital but not cutting-edge capability. BYD's battery dominance reflects vertical integration that Western firms cannot easily replicate. US special operations forces cannot modify their own equipment because contractors hold the keys. Europe's energy transition runs through American LNG infrastructure it doesn't control. Ukraine's ground robots reveal both the promise and vulnerability of unmanned systems when adversary drones can destroy them at will. The BlackCore investigation shows influence operations privatizing — states outsourcing information warfare to firms that exist in regulatory gray zones. The pattern is clear: technology concentrates power, and that concentration creates leverage points that adversaries can target, exploit, or simply wait out. The decisive strategic question is whether to accept dependency and manage risk, or to invest in the industrial base and regulatory capacity to build alternatives. Most actors are doing neither well.
sources: ABC News, Air Force Times, Defense News, Electrek, Forbes, Global Banking & Finance (Reuters), OilPrice.com (IEEFA), Seeking Alpha, 24/7 Wall St., Stanford AI Index 2026, SearXNG aggregation
